intro:
The gate of an ancient city was far more than an entrance — it was the civic, judicial, and commercial center of urban life. The gate complex typically included a wide open space just inside the entrance where elders sat, disputes were adjudicated, business was transacted, and public announcements were made. To sit in the gate was to exercise judicial authority (Ruth 4:1–11); to be crushed in the gate was to be publicly humiliated; to control the gate was to control the city. Jerusalem's multiple named gates — Sheep Gate, Fish Gate, Valley Gate — are documented in Nehemiah's rebuilding account.

In Scripture the gate carries theological significance at several levels. Entering the gates of Jerusalem with thanksgiving was an act of worship (Psalm 100:4). Lot sat in the gate of Sodom, marking his civic integration into a corrupt city (Genesis 19:1). The wide gate and broad way that leads to destruction are contrasted with the narrow gate and hard way that leads to life (Matthew 7:13–14) — one of Jesus's most compact theological contrasts. The gates of Hades, against which Christ promised his church would prevail (Matthew 16:18), evokes the gate as the seat of authority and power of the realm of death.
